system.docHtml:
Strength is the power of muscle and bone—the force that a person can bring to bear through physical exertion. It determines how much can be lifted, how far a stone can be thrown, how much a blow can accomplish. Strength is partly hereditary and partly the result of labor, but even the hardest-working miller cannot exceed the limits that strength sets for them.

Feeble

Feeble individuals possess little physical power. A feeble person tires quickly under exertion, cannot lift heavy burdens, and throws without force. They may be healthy and capable, but their muscles simply do not generate the power others take for granted. They suit themselves to labor that demands care rather than strength.

Weak

Weak folk lack the forceful quality that comes with true strength. They manage light labor and can lift a moderate burden, but heavy work exhausts them. A weak farmhand cannot swing a hammer with authority or pitch hay for a full day without rest. Their limits are real, though not desperate.

Average

Average strength suits most ordinary labor. A person of average strength can lift a sack of grain, swing an axe with reasonable force, and work a full day at physical tasks without collapse. Most folk possess such strength, which is adequate for the world's ordinary demands.

Forceful

Forceful individuals possess strength that is plainly above the ordinary. They lift heavy burdens easily, their blows land with authority, and they can labor all day and into the next. A forceful blacksmith accomplishes in an hour what an average one requires two for; a forceful sailor hauls rope with casual power.

Mighty

Mighty individuals are prodigies of strength—the sort of which tales are told. They lift what seems impossible, their blows could fell an ox, and their labor seems inexhaustible. Those of mighty strength are rare, and often used for the heaviest labor, though many find other applications for such power.

In Practice

When a merchant must move a barrel of wine from cart to cellar, the feeble person requires help simply to tilt it. The weak one manages to move it slowly and with effort, resting frequently. The average person carries it in a reasonable time. The forceful worker moves it without apparent strain, accomplishing the task as a side matter. The mighty worker carries it one-handed.

Endurance quantifies a person's physical stamina and constitutional health—the capacity to withstand hardship, resist disease, and labor without collapse. It is the accumulated vigor of body and blood. Those with keen endurance can work a harvest from dawn to dusk, march a week without rest, and survive wounds that would fell others. Those lacking it are fragile, susceptible to sickness, and quickly exhausted.

Frail

Frail individuals are physically delicate and prone to illness. Even light labor leaves them exhausted, and their bodies are vulnerable to every draft and contagion. A frail person cannot march far, cannot endure hardship, and tires quickly even in rest. They are often plagued by small ailments and may not reach old age.

Unfit

Unfit folk lack the vigor for heavy labor. They tire quickly under exertion, require frequent rest, and are susceptible to common sicknesses. An unfit person can perform their daily tasks but cannot manage extraordinary labor, and a bout of fever may lay them low for weeks. They are not inherently sickly but lack the stamina for hardship.

Average

Average persons possess the vigor one expects of healthy folk. They can labor a full day, resist ordinary illnesses adequately, and recover from minor wounds and sickness without undue struggle. They tire but do not collapse, sicken but do not die, and constitute the ordinary human norm.

Robust

Robust individuals seem physically hardy. They labor all day without excessive fatigue, rarely sicken, and recover quickly from illness or injury. A robust peasant works the fields from spring to autumn with unflagging vigor; a robust soldier endures the hardships of campaign without particular complaint.

Hardy

Hardy folk are prodigies of physical resilience. They seem unable to sicken, tire slowly even under extreme exertion, and recover from wounds that would cripple others. A hardy person can march for weeks, labor at mining depths, or endure naval voyage through foul weather, all while remaining strong. Such vigor inspires awe.

In Practice

When a harvest must be brought in before storm, the frail worker cannot participate; they would collapse. The unfit worker manages a few hours and must rest, returning slowly to work. The average laborer works from dawn to dusk with a brief meal-break, tired but managing. The robust worker labors tirelessly for the full period, requiring only normal food and sleep. The hardy worker seems to draw energy from the work itself, working a day and a night straight and awakening fresh to continue.

Dexterity measures the coordination between hand and eye, the steadiness of grip, and the reflexive quickness with which a person's limbs respond to intention. It is the difference between a fletcher who can split a quill lengthwise without shaking and one whose hands betray every tremor. High dexterity enables feats of precision; low dexterity makes clumsy havoc of delicate work.

Uncoordinated

The uncoordinated person's hands and eyes seem to work at cross purposes. A cup held firmly in one hand slips while they reach with the other. They drop things easily, mis-aim, and find detailed work exhausting. Their clumsiness is not mere carelessness; their very nerves refuse to obey with precision.

Awkward

Awkward individuals lack the grace of smooth coordination. They manage the tasks of daily life, but slowly and with effort. They are the ones who catch their foot on the stairs, who knock over a cup while reaching for bread, whose handwriting wavers and whose stitches are uneven.

Average

Average folk possess steady hands suited to ordinary work. They can stitch a seam, pen a fair line, and move through a crowded room without upending tables. They are capable and unremarkable, and most labor falls comfortably within their grasp.

Deft

Deft hands move with quiet precision. A deft scribe's letters are uniform and beautiful; a deft surgeon's cuts are clean and sure. They do not waste motion and seem to know where their fingers are without looking. Work that others find trying comes almost naturally to them.

Adroit

The adroit person possesses extraordinary hand-to-eye coordination. Their fingers seem to know what they need to do before conscious thought catches up. They juggle, they pick locks, they weave without studying their loom, and their hands move as though independent instruments of skill.

In Practice

When a master weaponsmith asks an apprentice to file the edge of a blade, the uncoordinated one leaves gouges and must begin again. The awkward youth files roughly, and the blade must be filed anew by someone with steadier hands. The average apprentice does adequate work. The deft one produces a honed edge that catches the light evenly. The adroit smith files with such economy of motion and sureness that the blade is ready for tempering in half the time it takes an ordinary craftsperson.

Agility is the quickness and fluidity of bodily movement—the capacity to move swiftly, to dodge, and to maintain balance and control. It differs from dexterity, which concerns hand-and-eye coordination, and from strength, which measures power. Agility is what allows a person to move through a crowded marketplace without stumbling, to survive a fall from a horse, and to strike quickly and smoothly.

Blundering

Blundering individuals move without grace or coordination of the larger body. They stumble over level ground, cannot catch themselves as they fall, and collide with obstacles. Their larger movements seem chaotic, as though their limbs betray their intentions. They are clumsy at climbing, hesitant at horseback, and generally look ill at ease in their own skin.

Clumsy

Clumsy folk move with less grace than ideal. They bump into things, recover awkwardly from stumbles, and move with visible effort. A clumsy person looks uncomfortable when asked to move quickly, and their larger gestures lack economy. They are not dangerous to themselves, but nor do they move with confidence.

Average

Average folk move adequately. They walk without stumbling, fall without serious damage, and possess normal reflexive stability. Most people are agile in this ordinary way—present in their bodies, capable of the movements daily life requires, but nothing more.

Nimble

Nimble individuals move with evident grace and control. They are quick and sure-footed, rarely stumble, and recover swiftly if they do. A nimble person leaps stairs two at a time, climbs easily, and dances well. They move through crowded rooms without apparent effort or consciousness.

Graceful

Graceful folk possess movement that is almost beautiful to behold. Every gesture is economical, every step flowing seamlessly into the next. A graceful warrior moves as though dancing, and a graceful dancer seems to float. Even mundane movements—rising from a chair, turning to answer—have an almost artistic quality.

In Practice

When a rope breaks beneath a person climbing a cliff, the blundering climber falls helplessly. The clumsy one makes a desperate grab and tumbles onto a ledge below. The average climber falls far but lands on stone somehow and survives maimed. The nimble climber catches themselves with a hand and stabilises on a hold. The graceful climber somehow twists mid-fall and lands running, barely bruised, as though they had planned for this eventuality.

Perception is the acuity of the senses—the keenness of eye, sharpness of ear, and sensitivity of nose. It determines who sees the hawk circling above when others see only sky, who hears the footstep on soft grass when others hear silence, and whose nose catches the scent of rot in the granary. A person with keen perception is difficult to surprise and deadly in the hunt.

Impaired

Impaired individuals struggle with their senses. Their eyes are weak, their ears dull, and their nose largely insensate. They miss what happens around them, not through inattention but through genuine sensory limitation. They may move through crowded rooms unaware of much happening, and hunting is nearly impossible for them.

Dulled

Dulled senses are less acute than normal. Such folk miss things that are plain to others. They do not hear a quiet conversation nearby, do not notice the small signs that precede a storm, and do not see movement in peripheral vision. They manage adequately in their own known spaces but are easily surprised elsewhere.

Average

Average perception is the norm. People see what is plainly before them, hear ordinary conversation, and smell smoke or decay. They notice marked changes in their surroundings but miss subtle signs. Most folk possess such perception.

Sharp

Sharp-eyed, sharp-eared folk notice details others miss. They see the flicker of movement in a distant wood, hear the whisper beneath normal conversation, and smell the old coin in a purse of new ones. Hunters of sharp perception rarely miss their prey; guards with such perception catch thieves red-handed.

Acute

Acute perception borders on the uncanny. Such individuals perceive details almost as though the world speaks directly to their senses. They can track by scent alone, spot a hidden figure in darkness, and hear the distress in another's voice beneath their calm words. Some believe such acuity comes from the Arcane itself.

In Practice

When a hunter tracks a deer through forest, the impaired one loses the trail immediately. The dulled tracker follows obvious disturbances but loses the scent where others would continue. The average hunter tracks competently but may miss the deer if it doubles back. The sharp-eyed tracker notices the bent grass and broken twigs, following surely. The acute tracker perceives the faint warmth of the recent track and hunts with almost uncanny directness.

Comeliness is the degree to which a person's physical form pleases the eye. It is more than mere health or vigor; it encompasses proportion, symmetry, and the indefinable grace that makes others wish to look upon a face or form. In taverns and courts alike, comeliness shapes first impressions and opens doors that plainer folk must knock upon more persistently.

Ugly

Ugly individuals bear features that instinctively repel. Their visage may be twisted by scar or deformity, their skin blotched or scarred beyond the ordinary marks of living. Others struggle not to look away. Some ugly folk cultivate a fearsome reputation to transform their apparent weakness into strength; others withdraw from scrutiny.

Unattractive

The unattractive person is unremarkable or vaguely displeasing to view. They lack obvious deformity but possess no particular grace. A crooked nose, narrow eyes, weak chin, or perpetually sour expression leaves them overlooked in crowds. They must rely on wit or skill to compensate for what the eye does not immediately favor.

Plain

Plain folk are neither remarkable nor off-putting. A miller's daughter, a shepherd lad—they pass through crowds without turning heads, possessing an honesty that avoids both beauty's burdens and ugliness's stigma. They are easy to forget but equally easy to trust.

Attractive

Attractive people draw the eye and hold it with ease. Their features show good proportion and their bearing suggests health. Others find themselves inclined to listen to their words, to grant them requests, to seek out their company. This favor comes without effort on their part, though it carries its own subtle weight.

Striking

The striking person is unforgettable. Their beauty—whether of sharp, severe design or soft, luminous quality—stops conversation in halls and creates sudden silences in markets. They become legendary in small towns, objects of gossip and longing. Some are blessed by this; others curse it as a prison that obscures all else about them.

In Practice

When a young person seeks passage on a merchant vessel, the captain barely glances at the plain applicant and asks for triple the usual fee. The attractive candidate finds the captain already offering a negotiated rate. The striking one is offered passage free—and the captain's hopeful company besides. By nightfall, the plain sailor has found work through tenacity; the attractive one through charm; the striking one has been offered a partnership and three marriage proposals, none of which they asked for.

Aura measures the depth and power of a person's soul—their fundamental presence in the world. Some individuals seem to carry an invisible weight, a thickness of being that others sense even without knowing why. Aura is particularly relevant to those touched by the Arcane, but even common folk may possess a deep or shallow aura. It shapes whether others feel drawn to or repelled by a person's presence, and it underlies all connexions to the forces of magic and fate.

Mundane

Mundane individuals seem thin and insubstantial in presence. They pass through the world leaving little impression. Arcane practitioners feel nothing from them, and even ordinary folk forget them easily. They do not seem to matter much, as though they are only half-present in the world.

Shallow

Shallow auras are those of ordinary, unremarkable folk. There is presence, but not much of it. Others notice them in the moment of interaction but forget them readily. They possess little natural authority and attract no particular attention from those sensitive to the Arcane.

Average

Average auras are precisely that—the norm for most living people. Such individuals are present and real without being memorable. They carry their own weight but nothing remarkable. They are invisible neither to Arcane perception nor memorable to casual encounter.

Deep

Deep auras suggest persons of considerable presence. Others feel it instinctively—a thickness of being, a quality of realness and importance. Such individuals command attention without demanding it, and those with Arcane sensitivity perceive them vividly. The world seems to respond to them.

Profound

Profound auras are rare—found only in persons whose souls seem to touch the very foundations of being. These individuals are unforgettable; they seem to belong to something larger than themselves. The Arcane sings in their presence, and common folk sense without understanding that they are in the presence of something significant.

In Practice

When a stranger enters a tavern, no one marks the mundane aura at all. The shallow-aured visitor is greeted perfunctorily and forgotten by morning. The average new arrival is made welcome as a normal guest. The deep-aured stranger draws eyes and conversation turns toward them naturally. The profound-aured figure may enter silently, yet the entire tavern grows quiet and attentive, as though the world itself had drawn breath to listen.

Will is the force of determination and self-mastery—the capacity to hold fast to a chosen course despite hardship, temptation, or fear. It governs whether a person falters when challenged or stands firm. Will is not the same as intelligence or strength; a fool may possess it, and a brilliant coward may lack it. It is the inner iron that separates those who bend from those who break.

Submissive

Submissive individuals offer little resistance to pressure or persuasion. They are easily swayed by force of personality, by threats, or even by gentle suggestion. They prefer to defer to others' judgment rather than trust their own conviction. Left to decide alone, they often choose the path of least resistance.

Indecisive

Indecisive folk are troubled by choice and struggle to commit to a course of action. They second-guess themselves constantly, waver between options, and are easily talked out of their own preferences. Under pressure, they freeze or shift toward whatever seems safest in the moment.

Average

Average persons possess ordinary resolve. They can stick to a modest commitment and endure minor hardship. They are neither easily shaken nor unusually steadfast. They do what they set out to do, most of the time, unless circumstances become very difficult.

Resolute

Resolute individuals are possessed of genuine determination. Once they have set their mind to something, they do not easily relent. They can endure hardship and disappointment without wavering. Others find them reliable and trustworthy, knowing that their word binds them even when the cost is high.

Implacable

The implacable person's will is like stone—immovable, absolute, and unwavering. Neither pain nor fear, temptation nor threat can turn them from what they have decided. This can make them admirable—the knight who refuses to yield, the widow who rebuilds after fire—or terrible—the tyrant who cannot conceive of mercy.

In Practice

When a young person leaves their village seeking fortune and the first season brings poverty, the submissive one returns home at the first suggestion from a friend. The indecisive person wanders between returning and staying, and ends up at someone else's mercy. The average youth endures long enough to find work. The resolute one works through hardship, taking any labor, and in five years has made their name. The implacable one not only endures but transforms hardship into legend.

Reasoning is the faculty of logic, memory, and deduction—the power to hold facts in mind, perceive patterns, and draw conclusions. It governs how quickly one grasps new ideas, whether one remembers the names of a merchant's children year after year, and whether one can unravel a knot of conflicting accounts to find the truth. It is the intellectual foundation upon which learning and craft are built.

Dim

Dim individuals struggle to follow complex thought. They forget conversations quickly, confuse details easily, and find abstract reasoning nearly impossible. They are not stupid in the sense of being incapable of basic tasks, but new ideas and complex problems cause them genuine distress. Memory is their greatest challenge.

Dull

Dull folk are slow to understand and slower still to retain what they have learned. They manage the routines of their lives through repetition and habit, but ask them something unexpected and their face clouds with effort. Difficult explanations exhaust them; complex problems are simply abandoned.

Average

People of average reasoning grasp most practical matters without undue strain. They remember faces and names reasonably well, follow conversations in hall or market, and can solve the ordinary problems that life presents. They are neither quick-witted nor forgetful, but reliably capable.

Sharp

Sharp minds seize upon ideas quickly and hold them securely. A sharp merchant remembers every transaction; a sharp scholar grasps the argument of a complex text on first reading. They spot inconsistencies, ask searching questions, and draw conclusions rapidly and usually correctly.

Brilliant

Brilliant individuals possess memory and deductive power that seems almost uncanny. They see patterns others miss, retain vast quantities of information effortlessly, and perceive the implications of a chain of reasoning before others have finished the first link. They can be difficult to live with, their impatience with slower minds barely concealed.

In Practice

When a lord describes a trade route through three foreign lands, the dim servant remembers only "foreign somewhere." The dull one remembers it goes to foreign lands but forgets the specific sequence. The average courtier recalls the route adequately. The sharp-minded apprentice asks exactly what goods pass where and why, and remembers it forever. The brilliant scholar has already calculated the profit margins by the time the lord finishes speaking and is prepared to suggest a better route entirely.

Creativity is the power of imagination—the capacity to perceive new connections, generate novel ideas, and forge original solutions to problems. It is what distinguishes the talented craftsperson from the merely competent, the visionary leader from the plodding administrator, the inspired poet from the mere versifier. Those with keen creativity see possibilities where others see only what is.

Banal

Banal individuals are utterly lacking in originality. Their thoughts are clichés, their suggestions are the most obvious ones, and they cannot conceive of doing anything except as it has always been done. Art from their hands is derivative; schemes they devise are pedestrian; they are the sort who bore dinner companions by relating the same tired stories.

Predictable

Predictable folk have some capacity for thought, but it runs along well-worn paths. They apply existing solutions to existing problems, never quite thinking sideways. A craftsperson of predictable creativity makes good serviceable goods but never improves the design. Conversations with them are dependable but uninspiring.

Average

Average individuals have modest creative capacity. They can improve a design slightly, solve an unusual problem by trial and error, and occasionally generate an idea worth listening to. Most people possess such creativity—enough to manage their lives with minor innovations but not so much that they seem strange.

Innovative

Innovative folk are those who see different ways of doing things. A smith of innovative spirit improves a tool's balance; a cook invents a new sauce; a builder suggests a stronger arch. They are sought out for their suggestions, and their improvements are usually sound, though occasionally spectacularly wrong.

Visionary

Visionaries perceive possibilities that others cannot imagine. They conceive of entirely new ways of working, new forms of art, new approaches to ancient problems. They are the ones who found the hidden trade route, designed the bridge that seemed impossible, or wrote the melody that was sung for generations. They are rare, and often misunderstood until history proves them right.

In Practice

When a carpenter is asked to design a new market stall, the banal one repeats the existing design exactly. The predictable one suggests minor variations. The average carpenter improves the drainage slightly. The innovative one reorganises the interior to hold fifty per cent more goods without additional footprint. The visionary suggests a design that changes how markets are built throughout the realm.

Empathy is the capacity to sense and understand the feelings, pain, and motives of others—to imagine oneself in another's circumstances and be moved by their plight. It shapes how a person relates to those around them, influencing their choices in moments of conflict or need. Those with keen empathy find themselves bound to others through genuine feeling; those lacking it move through the world with little regard for the inner lives of their fellows.

Unfeeling

The unfeeling person is all but deaf to the suffering of others. They observe emotion as one might watch clouds pass across the sky—with detachment and indifference. They are not necessarily cruel; they simply do not register the weight of another's tears or despair. Other people exist mostly as objects or obstacles in their path.

Remote

Remote individuals sense that others have feelings, but those feelings seem distant to them, almost theoretical. They understand the concepts of sorrow and joy in their minds but feel no answering resonance in their own breast. They may manage a functional courtesy but lack the warmth that comes from genuine connection.

Average

Those of average empathy grasp the feelings of those near to them and are stirred by extreme suffering, though their engagement fluctuates with circumstance and familiarity. They feel genuine sadness at a friend's loss yet remain largely unmoved by the hardships of strangers.

Understanding

Understanding folk carry a keen sensitivity to the emotional lives of others. They readily perceive pain and joy alike, and are moved to action by what they perceive. They listen well, remember kindnesses, and feel genuine connection to those they know. Their words often comfort, and their presence is sought.

Sensitive

The sensitive person's empathy is so acute that they feel the emotional weather of those around them almost as their own. They absorb others' suffering deeply and are sometimes overwhelmed by it. This may make them extraordinarily compassionate healers and counselors, or it may leave them brittle and exhausted from constant exposure to the world's pain.

In Practice

When a child weeps at the roadside, the unfeeling traveler rides past. The remote one slows, uncertain what is expected. The average traveler asks what is wrong, offers modest help, then continues. The understanding soul sits and listens, offers shelter and food, remembers the child's name. The sensitive traveler weeps alongside the child and feels their loss for weeks after.

Eloquence is the art of clear and persuasive speech—the capacity to choose the right words, frame them persuasively, and deliver them with confidence. It shapes how effectively a person can convince, inspire, teach, or deceive through language. An eloquent speaker is heard; an inarticulate one is often ignored even when speaking truth. Eloquence is not mere prettiness of language but the power of words to accomplish intention.

Incoherent

Incoherent speakers struggle to put thoughts into words. What seems clear in their mind comes out jumbled, contradictory, or impossible to follow. They grope for words, start sentences and abandon them, and leave listeners bewildered about what they meant. Others lose patience with them quickly, and their arguments fail not from being unsound but from being incomprehensible.

Muddled

Muddled speakers manage basic communication but lack clarity and force. Their words tumble out in no particular order, they repeat themselves without seeming to notice, and their points are obscured rather than illuminated. They can be understood if listeners are patient, but they are rarely persuasive and often seem foolish.

Average

Average folk speak clearly enough for ordinary purposes. Their meaning is plain, their words adequate, and listeners understand what they intend. They are neither articulate nor inarticulate, and most communication happens without difficulty. Most people speak in this ordinary fashion.

Articulate

Articulate speakers choose words with care and speak with a natural assurance. Their meaning is always clear, and there is a pleasantness to listening to them. They explain ideas effectively, teach well, and can present an argument such that listeners understand every point. They are sought out for counsel and believed readily.

Expressive

Expressive speakers seem to paint with words. They have a natural gift for the right phrasing, the apt metaphor, the telling detail. Listening to them is a pleasure; their arguments seem irresistible, and their teachings linger in the memory. They can move audiences to tears or laughter, inspire crusades, or persuade the unwilling. They are rare gifts in any community.

In Practice

When a merchant must convince a lord to grant trading rights, the incoherent one leaves the lord confused and irritated, the petition denied. The muddled speaker makes points but confuses the lord with contradictions, and the lord grants nothing. The average merchant presents adequately and receives a measured decision. The articulate merchant presents so clearly that the lord understands every advantage and grants provisional rights. The expressive merchant leaves the lord so convinced of mutual benefit that the lord offers partnership and funding besides.

Morality measures the depth and consistency of a character's ethical conscience—their capacity to distinguish right from wrong, and their willingness to act according to that discernment. It is the principle that governs choices in moments of temptation, ambition, or hardship. A person's moral fiber shapes not only individual decisions but also their reputation, the trust others place in them, and ultimately their place in community and faith alike.

Amoral

The amoral person has no internal ethical compass whatsoever. Concepts of right and wrong are abstractions to them, useful only insofar as they avoid punishment. Their choices arise purely from appetite, fear, or the desire for advantage. Such individuals are rare, and those who know them instinctively trust them with nothing of value.

Immoral

The immoral character knowingly chooses what they understand to be wrong, and does so with intent. They may delight in cruelty, actively deceive, or take what is not theirs—not out of ignorance, but out of deliberate malice or indulgence. The corrupt courtier, the faithless merchant, the blackmailer—all know the good they reject.

Hypocritical

Hypocrites profess virtue while harboring vices in private. They follow the outward forms of morality—paying lip service to honor, charity, or devotion—while their secret deeds contradict their words. Their conscience troubles them only when discovered, and they are swift to justify themselves by pointing to the failings of others.

Unscrupulous

The unscrupulous person cuts corners and bends rules with little remorse. They care moderately for right and wrong, but care far more for their own advancement or comfort. They will not murder for coin, but they will overcharge, lie on oath, or betray confidence if the risk seems small enough.

Conscientious

Conscientious folk genuinely seek to do right. They feel the weight of conscience keenly and are troubled by wrongdoing—both their own and others'. They make an earnest effort to keep their oaths, deal honestly, and live according to faith or code, though they may stumble through weakness or ignorance.

Principled

The principled person lives according to a clear and unwavering code. Their commitments are rooted in conviction, and they will endure hardship rather than compromise those commitments. They are trusted implicitly because their course is predictable and their word is inviolable.

Virtuous

The virtuous go beyond mere principle. Their ethical life is woven so thoroughly into their character that right action flows from them almost naturally. They seek the good not grudgingly but with grace, and they inspire others by example.

Paragon

Paragons of virtue are legends made flesh—individuals whose moral conviction and steadfast goodness reshape the world around them. Their very presence seems to call forth the better nature in those near them, and their sacrifices for principle become the stuff of tales.

In Practice

Consider a merchant faced with the chance to adulterate wine and pocket the difference. The amoral merchant sees only profit. The immoral one delights in the deception. The hypocrite might do it secretly while ostentatiously funding a chapel. The unscrupulous one might dilute just slightly, rationalizing that the buyer will not notice. The conscientious merchant feels genuine conflict but ultimately refuses, accepting the loss. The principled merchant refuses absolutely and makes a show of it. The virtuous merchant refuses and helps the buyer find good wine at a fair price elsewhere. The paragon's generosity becomes known throughout the shire, and a rival competitor, shamed, begins keeping honest measure as well.

Voice is the musical quality of a person's speech—the timbre, pitch, and natural resonance of the throat and lungs. A pleasant voice draws listeners in and makes words seem wise; a harsh or grating voice undermines even truth. Beyond mere pleasantness, voice carries character: the husky contralto of a weathered sea-captain, the clear soprano of a chapel singer, or the booming bellow of a blacksmith in his forge.

Jarring

Jarring voices offend the ear and cause actual discomfort. They may be shrill, harsh, or unpleasantly nasal—whatever the cause, prolonged listening fatigues and irritates. People unconsciously recoil from such voices, and speakers blessed with them must work twice as hard to be heard fairly.

Tuneless

Tuneless voices lack any particular grace or charm. They do not offend, exactly, but they command no attention. Words spoken in a tuneless voice seem to slide off listeners' ears unregistered, as though the voice itself refuses to carry their import.

Average

Average voices are serviceable and unremarkable. They carry words adequately, rising and falling with normal inflection. Listeners hear them without distraction, for better or worse. Most folk possess such voices.

Melodious

Melodious voices are a pleasure to hear. They possess natural warmth and carry persuasive power. A melodious speaker is readily believed; a melodious singer draws audiences willingly. People find themselves wanting to listen, and words spoken in such a voice seem to carry added weight.

Dulcet

Dulcet voices are rare gifts—so beautiful and rich that they seem almost uncanny. They draw listeners as honey draws bees. A dulcet voice can sell goods that should not sell, persuade hearts that should resist, and inspire passion. People are often more swayed by the voice than by what is actually said.

In Practice

When a merchant calls out his wares in the marketplace, the jarring voice's words seem harsh and unwelcome, and few approach his stall. The tuneless voice goes unheard in the general noise. The average merchant's cry blends into the market's noise. The melodious voice stands out pleasantly, and customers pause to listen. The dulcet voice stops traffic—people abandon their business to hear it, and the merchant's ordinary cloth becomes suddenly desirable.

The human is a creature of remarkable adaptability and unremarkable physicality. Humans are neither the fastest nor the strongest, neither perfectly built for any single environment nor specialized for any singular purpose. Yet they thrive everywhere the world offers purchase: in deserts and ice, in mountains and forests, in cities of stone and settlements of leather and thatch. What other lineages inherit through biological perfection, humans achieve through ingenuity, cooperation, and relentless will to persist where other creatures would fail.

Description

Humans are bipedal primates standing five to six and a half feet tall, weighing one hundred to three hundred pounds, remarkably variable in appearance across their numerous cultural groups and geographic populations. Human adaptability is their defining characteristic—skin coloration ranges from pale to deep brown depending on ancestral adaptation to sunlight exposure; body structure varies from lean and swift to heavily muscled; facial features, hair coloration, and body composition are equally variable. This variation is not weakness but strength—it reflects humanity's capacity to thrive across climates and environments that would specialize other creatures into narrow niches.

Humans are not biologically perfect for any single purpose. They are not as strong as elephants or dragons, not as swift as onagers, not as naturally armored as insects. Instead, they possess cognitive capacity and social organization that allows them to accomplish collectively what they lack individually.

Key Behaviors

Humans are social creatures organizing into complex societies ranging from small nomadic bands to sprawling civilizations spanning multiple nations and cultures. They are uniquely capable of abstract thought, long-term planning, and cooperation at scales that exceed any other lineage. They form emotional attachments, develop elaborate systems of morality and belief, create art and philosophy, and pursue knowledge for its own sake. They are capable of genuine compassion and equally capable of profound cruelty, often displaying both simultaneously depending on context and circumstance.

Humans reproduce relatively rapidly compared to long-lived lineages, reaching adulthood in their mid-teens to early twenties. They are motivated by complex desires—not merely survival but belonging, achievement, legacy, understanding, and meaning. They form permanent pair-bonds more consistently than most creatures, building families and dynasties that span generations.

Relations

Humans are the dominant lineage of Thalorna, establishing civilizations across most habitable regions and shaping the world through agriculture, construction, warfare, and trade. Most human cultures are pragmatic in their interactions with other lineages—domesticating and breeding creatures for work and food; hunting wild populations; negotiating with sapient creatures when mutual benefit is apparent. Some cultures hold particular lineages sacred—worshipping dragons as divine, respecting the elder races as superior, revering wilderness predators as spiritual embodiments.

The relationship between humans and the elder races—Sinalë (elves) and Khazári (dwarves)—is complex and historically fraught, involving periods of conflict, alliance, trade, and mutual respect. Humans view dreadspawn and helspawn (undead) as existential threats requiring elimination. Most humans fear or respect dragons depending on regional mythology. Some humans bond with individual creatures of other lineages, forming partnerships that become legendary.

Special Abilities

Cultural Diversity

Humans possess remarkable capacity to adapt culturally to any environment—developing specialized knowledge, tools, and practices allowing thrival in deserts, mountains, frozen wastes, and dense forests. Different human cultures emphasize different values and practices, collectively demonstrating approaches to nearly every conceivable form of social organization.

Cooperative Organization

Humans can organize in numbers and complexity that exceed other lineages—coordinating armies, constructing massive projects, pursuing complex goals requiring generations of effort. This organizational capacity allows humans to accomplish feats no individual human could achieve.

Adaptability and Innovation

Humans are characterized by capacity to develop new tools, techniques, and approaches to problems. They learn from experience, modify behavior based on observed results, and improve on inherited techniques. This adaptive capacity allows humans to thrive in new environments and overcome challenges that would specialize other creatures into failure.

Archery is the discipline of using hand-drawn stringed weapons—primarily bows and crossbows—to strike distant targets. It demands keen eyesight, steady hands, and years of dedicated practice to achieve true proficiency. Crossbows are somewhat more forgiving for untrained users, but mastering either weapon type at range remains a considerable achievement. The skill encompasses not merely aiming and releasing, but understanding windage, elevation, and the characteristics of different projectile types.

Awareness measures how attuned a character is to their physical surroundings—the rustle of movement in undergrowth, the glint of metal in shadow, or the faint scent of smoke on the wind. It is most commonly tested in situations where a character might passively notice something unusual, though it can also be actively employed when deliberately scanning for threats or hidden objects. Awareness stands in direct opposition to Stealth, forming the fundamental contest between the hidden and the vigilant. Deficiencies in a particular sense may limit or preclude certain tests, while heightened alertness greatly improves the chance of detecting danger.

Charm represents the art of emotional persuasion—winning others over through personal magnetism, warmth, and an appeal to their feelings rather than their reason. It draws heavily on physical attractiveness and the ability to read another person's mood, making it as much about intuition as technique. A charming individual can coax cooperation, defuse hostility, or entice agreement from those who might otherwise refuse. However, targets may resist through sheer force of will or see through the seduction by discerning its underlying motive.

Climbing covers the ability to scale vertical or near-vertical surfaces, from scrambling up a tree or low wall to ascending sheer cliff faces using only natural handholds. Easy climbs are resolved quickly as part of normal movement, while more demanding ascents require sustained effort and careful technique. Factors such as the angle of incline, availability of holds, and surface conditions all affect the difficulty. Falls from height carry serious risk of injury, making this a skill where failure can be catastrophic.

Command is the art of forceful persuasion through authority, intimidation, and sheer presence. Where Charm coaxes, Command compels—demanding obedience or compliance through the weight of one's bearing and voice. It is the tool of officers rallying troops, lords issuing decrees, and interrogators breaking resistance. Targets may resist through willpower or, when facing direct physical intimidation, through their own combat readiness and nerve.

Cookery is the practical art of preparing food—transforming raw ingredients into meals that nourish, satisfy, and sometimes delight. A skilled cook understands the properties of different foodstuffs, the timing of heat and seasoning, and how to make the most of whatever provisions are available. The quality of a meal affects not only the health of those who eat it but can influence the mood and social dynamics of a gathering. Truly dreadful cooking may cause illness, while exceptional fare enhances the host's standing and persuasive power.

Dancing encompasses the performance of formal and folk dances appropriate to one's culture and social station. Proficiency is measured over the course of an extended performance and reflects grace, timing, and familiarity with the steps and music. A skilled dancer can elevate a social occasion and win favor, while a clumsy or stilted performance may embarrass both dancer and host. Dance performances often influence the social dynamics of the events where they occur.

Discourse is the art of structured, logical persuasion—convincing a listener through reasoned argument, evidence, and rhetorical skill rather than emotional appeal or deception. It is the tool of scholars, lawyers, and diplomats who must present a compelling case before critical audiences. When two skilled debaters clash, the depth of relevant knowledge each brings to the topic becomes as important as their rhetorical technique. Discourse stands apart from other persuasion skills in its reliance on intellect and the strength of one's position.

Drawing covers the visual arts—painting, sketching, fresco work, and pen-and-ink illustration—in whatever style prevails in the character's culture. While anyone can make crude marks, true skill produces works of artistic merit or, in the case of maps and technical diagrams, practical accuracy. The quality of a drawing reflects both the creator's technical facility and their creative vision. Drawing also serves as the basis for executing heraldic designs and other works requiring precise visual representation.

Folklore represents a character's knowledge of their homeland's culture, history, and traditions. Scholars typically organize this body of knowledge into broad categories: geography, history, the natural and supernatural world, myth and legend, poetry and literature, and social customs. A Folklore test may determine whether a character can recall a specific piece of information or gauge their general depth of understanding about a broad topic. Knowledge of foreign lands diminishes sharply with distance, making well-traveled individuals and those with access to libraries particularly valued as sources of information.

Guile is the art of deliberate deception—convincing others of something the speaker knows to be false. Unlike Theatrics, which involves assuming a false identity or persona, Guile operates through distortion of facts while the deceiver remains ostensibly themselves. Its applications range from a merchant misrepresenting wares to a spy feeding disinformation, from a litigant misleading a court to a card player bluffing opponents. Deception is resisted through Intrigue, the complementary skill of detecting falsehood.

Initiative reflects a combatant's battlefield experience, tactical awareness, and mental discipline under the pressure of violent confrontation. While everyone possesses some basic survival instinct, true Initiative develops through training and direct participation in combat. It governs the order in which combatants act during an engagement and is tested when fighters must gauge their own readiness and fighting spirit. The Shock subskill, which measures resilience to physical trauma, shares Initiative's training progression while drawing on the body's raw toughness.

Shock represents a combatant's physiological resilience to the trauma of violent injury—the ability to absorb a blow without being incapacitated by pain, blood loss, or the body's involuntary shutdown responses. It is tested whenever a character suffers significant physical damage in combat, determining whether they can continue to fight or are overcome by the shock of their wounds. Conditioning, body mass, and raw endurance all contribute to this capacity, which can mean the difference between staying on one's feet and collapsing from a wound that a tougher individual might shrug off.

Intrigue is the ability to perceive hidden motives, detect deception, and sense the undercurrents of conspiracy in social interactions. It operates on two scales: the personal, where one reads an individual's true intentions behind their words and manner, and the broader political, where one unravels plots and schemes within organizations or communities. Both applications rely on emotional perceptiveness and rational analysis working in concert. Intrigue serves as the primary defense against Guile and Theatrics, making it indispensable for anyone navigating the dangerous waters of court politics or criminal enterprise.

Jumping measures a character's ability to leap distances both horizontally and vertically, whether crossing a ditch, vaulting a low wall, or reaching an overhead handhold. A running start significantly extends the distance achievable, while vertical leaps allow a character to grasp objects above their normal reach. Miscalculated jumps risk stumbles and falls, making it a skill where confidence must be tempered by realistic assessment of one's limits.

Melee governs the effective use of hand-held weapons in close combat—swords, axes, maces, polearms, and all other arms wielded in direct confrontation with an opponent. It measures not just the ability to swing a weapon but the tactical awareness to exploit openings, the footwork to maintain advantageous positioning, and the instinct to read an opponent's intentions. Proficiency develops through training and live combat experience, with raw physical attributes of coordination and agility forming the foundation upon which technique is built.

Dodge represents the instinctive ability to evade incoming attacks in combat by shifting one's body out of the path of a blow or missile. Unlike active parrying with a weapon or shield, dodging relies purely on reflexes, spatial awareness, and the ability to read an opponent's intent. It is a fundamental defensive skill available to all combatants regardless of armament, though it becomes increasingly difficult against skilled or multiple attackers.

Physician encompasses the medical knowledge needed to diagnose illness, treat injuries, perform surgery, and manage the long-term care of the sick and wounded. It is one of the most demanding of the learned professions, requiring both theoretical understanding of the body and practical skill in applying treatments. A physician's effectiveness in treating wounds and diseases depends on both their medical expertise and the quality of available resources. The skill covers battlefield triage as well as the more deliberate practice of a settled healer's craft.

Riding governs the ability to control a mounted animal—typically a horse, but potentially a mule, camel, or more exotic beast—in a variety of situations from casual travel to the chaos of mounted combat. The skill encompasses not only balance and posture in the saddle but the subtle communication between rider and mount through leg, hand, and voice commands. An empathic connection with the animal underlies all effective riding, and the most accomplished riders seem to act as a single entity with their mount.

Singing measures vocal performance ability—the control of pitch, tone, volume, and phrasing that transforms the human voice into a musical instrument. Every culture has its own repertoire of songs for work, worship, celebration, and mourning, and a skilled singer can move audiences to emotion, enhance ceremonial occasions, and earn social standing through their art. Natural vocal quality provides a foundation, but true excellence requires training and creative interpretation.

Stealth is the art of moving unseen and unheard—the ability to exploit shadows, cover, and ambient noise to avoid detection. It encompasses sneaking past sentries, shadowing a target through crowded streets, and concealing oneself in the natural environment. Stealth is directly opposed by Awareness in a fundamental contest between the hidden and the alert, with environmental conditions such as lighting, terrain, and background noise playing a significant role in determining the outcome.

Survival encompasses the practical knowledge needed to endure in the wilderness—finding shelter, locating water, foraging for edible plants, navigating by natural signs, and coping with the hazards of weather, terrain, and wildlife. It is the skill of the frontier scout, the lost traveler, and anyone who must sustain themselves far from the comforts of civilization. While some basic survival instincts are nearly universal, true competence in the wild demands determination, keen senses, and hard-won experience.

Swimming governs the ability to move through water under one's own power, whether crossing a river, surviving a shipwreck, or diving beneath the surface. Proficiency ranges from the desperate flailing of someone who can barely stay afloat to the confident strokes of an experienced swimmer who can cover long distances in rough conditions. Encumbrance from armor and equipment dramatically affects swimming ability, and even strong swimmers can be overwhelmed by powerful currents or exhaustion.

Theatrics is the art of performance and disguise—the ability to assume a role, alter one's apparent identity, and sustain a convincing persona through voice, mannerism, and bearing. Unlike Guile, which distorts facts while the speaker remains ostensibly themselves, Theatrics involves wholesale transformation of how one presents to the world. Applications range from stage performance and storytelling to espionage and infiltration. A skilled practitioner can adopt accents, mimic social classes, and project emotions they do not feel, though Intrigue remains the primary defense against even the most polished deception.

Throwing governs the ability to hurl objects—daggers, hand axes, javelins, stones, and other improvised or purpose-made projectiles—with accuracy and force. While less powerful than archery at extended ranges, thrown weapons offer the advantage of quick deployment and require less specialized equipment. Coordination and perception are the primary physical attributes involved, and skilled throwers can achieve surprising accuracy even with objects not designed as weapons.

A silver coin stamped with the royal mark, the everyday currency of trade in markets and taverns. Smaller than the gold crown but more durable, the pence serves the merchant, farmer, and laborer equally. The coin's standard weight and purity ensure acceptance in any realm that honors the crown's seal.
